title: State
scan: What you will learn: What state is, the equation that governs every modern UI framework, and how to design a state model that makes bugs auditable rather than ephemeral
Why it matters: State bugs are the hardest to reproduce because they are temporal — the state at the time of the bug is gone — a disciplined state model makes the system auditable
Current State + Event = New State — every framework from Redux to Angular Signals is an elaboration of that equation
The single source of truth is not a performance optimisation but a correctness constraint — duplicate state always diverges
UI state and application state are different concerns and must be stored in different places — conflating them is the root cause of most "the UI doesn't match the data" bugs

blocks.html: very modern application reduces to one equation. It fits on a napkin:

State machines pay off at scale. A component with 4 boolean flags (isLoading, isError, isEmpty, isSuccess) has 16 possible states, most of which are invalid and untested. A state machine with 4 named states (idle, loading, ready, error) has exactly 4 — each reachable only via defined transitions. Invalid states become impossible.

Storing the result of a filter operation in state. When the source array updates, the filtered result is stale. Symptom: a user clears a filter and sees old results until the next full fetch. Fix: store the source array and the filter parameters; recompute the filtered view on every render.

sections.title: State — what the system remembers between moments

blocks.html: State — what the system remembers between moments
Every modern application reduces to one equation. It fits on a napkin:

That equation is why React, Redux, MobX, Zustand, Angular Signals — every modern frontend framework — exists. They are all elaborations of three words: state plus event. Get the equation right and every framework becomes legible at a glance.

State is everything the system has to remember between moments. Variables hold individual values; state is the aggregate that defines what the user is currently looking at, who they are, where they are in a flow, and what the system is doing on their behalf.

items: Authentication state — who is logged in and what they can see.
Application state — the overall mode the app is in (loading, ready, error, offline).
Workflow state — where the user is in a multi-step process.
Page state — what's on screen right now, what's selected, what's open.
Domain state — cart contents, search results, the current document.

blocks.html: State must live in one place. Render reads from it. Biz writes to it. API hydrates it. The moment two places hold the "same" piece of state, they drift — and the rest of the project is spent chasing why one screen shows old data.
If you can't answer "where does this value live?" in one breath, your state is fragmented.

This is why Stalwart (Module 09 onwards) treats state.js as a first-class file — distinct from model.js. The model owns the shape of an entity; state owns the current value of that entity in the running app.

blocks.label: Architect's Review · State
items: Who owns each piece of state?
Is there a single source of truth, or are copies drifting?
